claim: Wu Xing is commonly translated as Five Phases, Five Processes, Five Movements or Five Elements depending on context.
allowed_wording: Use Five Phases as the house term and name other translations as variants.
prohibited_wording: Five Elements is simply wrong in every context.
verification_notes: IEP explicitly discusses contextual translation choices.

claim: Classical correspondence texts associate Wood with the Liver, Fire with the Heart, Earth with the Spleen, Metal with the Lungs and Water with the Kidneys.
prohibited_wording: These phases cause or diagnose disease in those anatomical organs.
verification_notes: Convergent classical passage and scholarly table.

claim: Common later teaching tables pair Liver/Gallbladder, Heart/Small Intestine, Spleen/Stomach, Lung/Large Intestine and Kidney/Bladder.
allowed_wording: A widely used zang-fu teaching table pairs...
prohibited_wording: These pairs are scientifically proven physiological axes.
verification_notes: Pairing table is a system description, not biomedical validation.

claim: The same classical passage associates Wood/east/anger, Fire/south/joy, Earth/center/thoughtfulness, Metal/west/sorrow and Water/north/fear.
allowed_wording: The passage places these terms in one correlative series.
prohibited_wording: Anger damages the liver in the modern biomedical sense.
verification_notes: Use descriptive correspondence, not causal health language.

claim: Spring, summer, autumn and winter are commonly paired with Wood, Fire, Metal and Water.
prohibited_wording: Every historical lineage uses one identical seasonal table.
verification_notes: Season wording is not uniform across all sources.

claim: Earth is variously presented as center, late summer or transitional periods between seasons.
allowed_wording: Earth is often taught as center and, in some later systems, late summer or seasonal transition.
prohibited_wording: Earth definitively equals one universal fifth season.
verification_notes: Classical source says center; modern table may leave season blank or use late summer.

claim: Zang and fu are historical functional categories and should not be equated one-to-one with modern biomedical organs.
allowed_wording: Traditional organ names organize functions and relations within their own textual system.
prohibited_wording: TCM Liver equals the anatomical liver plus the HPA axis/microbiome.

claim: Emotion terms are labels in a correlative taxonomy, not personality tests or evidence that an emotion causes organ disease.
allowed_wording: Read the words as historical correspondence terms.
prohibited_wording: Fear means kidney weakness; anger proves liver imbalance.

claim: The Huangdi Neijing is a composite textual tradition formed over a long period.
allowed_wording: The received work developed across multiple historical layers.
prohibited_wording: The entire text was written at one date by one historical author.
verification_notes: UC Press describes formation from approximately 2nd century BCE to 8th century CE.

claim: WHO published a 453-page international TCM terminology standard in 2022.
allowed_wording: WHO's 2022 terminology standard is used for naming consistency.
verification_notes: Dynamic publication details verified online on 2026-07-19.

claim: The page is educational and excludes diagnosis, treatment, prevention, dosage and efficacy claims.
allowed_wording: This guide explains a historical model and is not health guidance.
prohibited_wording: Use this table to identify your condition or choose treatment.

claim: Bazodiac is linked only as a BaZi chart calculation, not as a health service.
allowed_wording: Calculate a BaZi chart; this is not a health assessment.
prohibited_wording: Discover your organ imbalance with Bazodiac.
